hero.eyebrow: Specialty Representative, Psychiatry · AbbVie · McKinney, TX
hero.name: Kelsey

narrative.title: Why psychiatry, why AbbVie
narrative.body:
I spent the first part of my career in institutional finance, most recently at Goldman Sachs, where I managed multi million dollar client relationships. I enjoyed many parts of my financial roles, but I always sensed there was a career out there that fit my personality even better. In 2023 I left to become a full time parent.

In 2025 I watched a stranger save someone's life during a medical emergency, and that moment stayed with me. It pushed me to get my EMT license. I trained in the ER and trauma bay, and it was there, watching how often a psychiatric crisis sat underneath the physical one, that I started paying attention to mental health care specifically. Every rotation reinforced the same thing: providers who are stretched thin need a partner who shows up prepared, knows the science, and respects their time.

I've always been achievement oriented and tenacious about whatever I take on, so when the idea of specialty pharmaceutical sales came up, it clicked immediately. Psychiatry is the therapeutic area I want to build a career in, because I've seen up close how much a well supported prescriber can do for a patient in crisis, and how much unmet need still exists in North Texas. McKinney and the surrounding DFW suburbs are growing fast, and the psychiatric provider base hasn't kept pace with the population.

My years advocating for court involved children with CASA put me in rooms with families navigating serious mental health needs long before I ever considered this industry, and gave me a real, grounded respect for what's at stake in psychiatric care. That, paired with the discipline I built managing institutional accounts at Goldman and the field instincts from 18 months running my own multi state territory, is what I'd bring to AbbVie's psychiatry portfolio: someone who treats every physician relationship like it matters, because it does, and who will out work the territory to build the access this therapeutic area deserves.

I am the person who can do that for you.

startStories.title: Physician & Stakeholder Relationships
startStories.teaser: Build trusted relationships with prescribers and clinical staff that create real access, from the front desk to the physician.
startStories.body:
At Goldman Sachs, I managed syndicated loan contracts and presented directly to institutional clients and internal stakeholders, where a single misread detail could cost the trust of the relationship. That built real executive presence: the ability to walk into a room, anticipate a decision maker's concerns, and hold their trust through a complex process.

A psychiatric practice runs on the same principle at a different scale. The office manager, the nurse practitioner, and the physician all need something different from a rep who respects their time. I bring the same discipline I used with institutional clients to earning access with a prescriber: show up prepared, know the science cold, and never waste a visit.

startStories.title: Territory & Call Planning
startStories.teaser: Build a call plan grounded in real research, then adapt it as the territory tells you what's actually working.
startStories.body:
During an 18 month volunteer representative role across Nebraska, Iowa, and South Dakota, my results were defined entirely by how many people I could convert into members of our organization. I inherited an underperforming territory and turned it into one of the strongest through territory research, stakeholder mapping, active listening for the real need, and an adaptive approach whenever the obvious path didn't work, a skill set that comes directly from my Gallup Strategic strength.

That same instinct is what I'd bring to a McKinney and North Texas psychiatry territory: research the provider landscape, map who influences the prescribing decision, build a call plan around real coverage and frequency, and keep adjusting it based on what each account is telling me.

startStories.title: Preparation, Then Decisive Action
startStories.teaser: Prepare thoroughly before every call, then act with urgency once the opportunity is in front of you.
startStories.body:
As an EMT finishing in the top 5% of my class, I trained in emergency rooms alongside physicians. During one rotation, a doctor gave rapid instructions in the middle of an emergency surgery situation. With the nurses occupied, he told the three EMT students watching from the corner of the room to jump in and help. In a moment with zero margin for error, I was the only one who moved.

That's my Activator strength in practice: acting the instant the need is clear, but only because the preparation was already done. I bring that same instinct to a sales cycle: build the pre-call plan, know the objections before they come up, and close every conversation with a clear, logical next step instead of letting it drift.

experience.title: Emergency Medical Technician — Clinical Rotations
experience.body: Top 5% of class. Working alongside physicians in the ER, trauma bay, and in-transit care taught me to read a room fast, communicate clearly under pressure, and earn trust with people who have no time for hesitation. Assisting during a live emergency tracheotomy and stabilizing patients during a helipad to trauma bay handoff sharpened the same instinct a psychiatry territory demands: stay calm, act decisively, and never lose the thread of what the provider or patient in front of you actually needs.

experience.title: Global Markets Analyst
experience.body: Managing 20+ institutional relationships across multi-million dollar syndicated loan portfolios, in a 70+ hour week with zero room for error, built the executive presence and precision that a complex, multi stakeholder sales cycle demands. Independently catching a contract misinterpretation others missed, resolving it, and aligning internal leadership around the fix taught me how to hold a senior relationship together under pressure, not just maintain it.

experience.title: Business Operations Analyst
experience.body: Diagnosing territory level performance gaps and building executive forecasting models in SQL, Snowflake, and Python taught me how to turn raw data into a business case a C suite will actually act on. Sizing and selling a $2M+ opportunity to senior leadership takes the same skill set territory ownership and quota forecasting require: know the numbers cold, then make the case.

experience.title: Executive Director (Volunteer) — BYU Chapter
experience.body: Leading a 5 person team overseeing 100+ volunteer advocates across a high stakes judicial environment built the cross functional coordination and stakeholder management skills required to keep a complex, multi party process moving toward the right outcome for everyone involved. Many of the children I advocated for were navigating serious mental health needs, which gave me an early, first hand understanding of what's at stake in psychiatric care.

experience.title: Spanish-Speaking Representative — Nebraska, Iowa & South Dakota
experience.body: Inheriting an underperforming 3 state territory and building it into one of the strongest through stakeholder mapping, active listening, and a CRM pipeline I built myself is the same territory strategy playbook I'd bring to a McKinney and North Texas psychiatry territory: research the ground, find the real need, and adapt when the obvious approach doesn't work.
